---
title: "Sports Analytics Using Ridge/Lasso Regression Summer 2026"
subtitle: "Predicting Team Wins Based On QB Play"
author: "Jonathan Sears, Jasmine Cole, Noah Miller"
date: '`r Sys.Date()`'
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
course: Capstone Projects in Data Science
bibliography: references.bib # file contains bibtex for references
#always_allow_html: true # this allows to get PDF with HTML features
self-contained: true
execute: 
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

Slides: [slides.html](slides.html){target="_blank"} ( Go to `slides.qmd`
to edit)

::: callout-important
**Remember:** Your goal is to make your audience understand and care
about your findings. By crafting a compelling story, you can effectively
communicate the value of your data science project.

Carefully read this template since it has instructions and tips to
writing!
:::

## Introduction

Under the stadium lights, the smell of popcorn and hot dogs and the band
playing the “Fight song” as one final play runs the last 3 seconds of
the 4th quarter, that is the aesthetic of an American Football game.
Fans come together to support, cheer and hope for their team's victory.
But what if fans or people in general had the ability to go into a match
where they could predict the outcome? Being able to predict outcomes in
the National Football League (NFL) requires many different features and
data such as home field advantage, player performance, team statistics,
and game conditions which can cause “multicollinearity”.
Multicollinearity is the negative impact on the least square estimator
in regression due to correlation between variables
[@mcdonald2009ridge]. 

With the implementation of a machine learning regression model such as
Lasso  (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator), and Ridge we
can simplify the variables and focus only on the important aspects that
help to accurately predict wins. Lasso uses a shrinkage technique called
L1 regularization where it zeros out the least important variables,
leaving only variables that are optimal for accurate prediction. Ridge
regression uses a shrinkage technique called L2 regularization where it
helps reduce multicollinearity without removing variables from the
original set of independent variables. This helps with some of the
distortion of the predictors. 

Previous research examining LASSO regression has consistently
demonstrated its usefulness in datasets containing large numbers of
predictors and correlated variables. Across applications in sports
analytics, genetics, and other predictive modeling fields, LASSO has
been shown to improve model interpretability by shrinking insignificant
coefficients toward zero while retaining the most influential predictors
[@bhattacharyya2025lasso; @jana2021football; @lockhart2014lasso]. Such
reduces model complexity and can improve predictive performance compared
to traditional regression methods [@alshqaq2023diagnostic;
@bhattacharyya2025lasso; @lockhart2014lasso]. Studies have also shown
that regularization techniques such as Ridge regression can effectively
address multicollinearity by reducing coefficient variance without
removing predictors from the model. One limitation discussed within the
literature is that highly correlated variables can influence LASSO's
variable selection process, potentially causing relevant predictors to
be excluded [@lockhart2014lasso]. Overall, the findings suggest that
regularized regression methods provide a useful framework for prediction
problems involving numerous and potentially correlated variables, making
them applicable to NFL team performance analysis.

The main goal of this report is to examine the impact of
multicollinearity on predicting NFL team success and to examine whether
regularized regression models are better than a linear regression model.
This will be seen by comparing a linear regression, Ridge regression,
and Lasso regression ability to predict the number of regular-season
wins by an NFL team using that team’s quarterback's performance
statistics. Some of the predictor variables are the quarterback’s season
passing yards, touchdowns, interceptions and passing expected points 
added (EPA), a metric that measures how many net points a quarterback
adds to their team's scoring potential on passing plays, compared to the
league-average expectation for any given down, distance, and field
position. This report will also aim to find which quarterback
performance statistics are most influential in predicting team
performance. Understanding the impact of a quarterback’s performance on
a team's success and the most influential quarterback performance
statistics provide valuable information for NFL analysts, coaches, and
teams trying to find players that contribute to team success and improve
team performance.

## Methods

Across various predictive modeling applications ranging to sports
outcomes in (South & Egros, 2020), sensor organization to ensure
optimization (Laufer et al., 2023) and credit risks awareness in
(Geraldo-Campos et al., 2022) LASSO, least absolute shrinkage and
selection operator and ridge regression are both linear regression
technique and methodologies that are designed to handle
multicollinearity and mult-dimensional data. Both of these methodologies
use a shrinkage tool that penalizes the regression coefficients. Both
methods solve a modified optimization problem of the form:
![](data:image/png;base64,)

\

where y is the response vector, X is the matrix of predictors, is the
vector of the coefficients, P()is the penalty term, and 0 is the
regularization parameter controlling the strength of the penalty. 

However, the difference between LASSO and Ridge regression comes from
how their penalties are calculated mathematically. LASSO uses a L1
penalty norm, which shrinks the least important variables in the dataset
to zero which allows for the selection of key effective variables 

(P()=j-1pj). Ridge regression uses a L2 penalty norm, which shrinks
parameters close to zero reducing the variance (P()= j=1pj2) but where
ridge differs from LASSO is that in the end and final model, ridge
retains all variables . LASSO is a “step-up” from ridge allowing for key
variable selection, where ridge can struggle with its limitations due to
retention of all variables. This highlights that LASSO’s ability to
produce a subset of “key-variables” from a high-dimensional dataset,
makes it highly efficient. 

For example, sports outcomes in (South & Egros, 2020), LASSO was able to
identify home-field advantage as a key-variable by narrowing down
recruitment and box-score metrics. Similarly in (Laufer et al., 2023)
LASSO was able to significantly reduce computational complexity that
identified an optimal configuration of motion sensors. However it is
important to note that LASSO has experienced some limitations in its
performance ability in variable selection specifically with highly
correlated variables and complex non-linear interactions. For example in
(Tarun & Sriramya, 2026), this study used lasso regression against
XGBoost to determine which methodology proved to be the most accurate at
predicting fare prices for online cab services which XGBoost
outperformed LASSO because “LASSO failed to make the grouped selection
as it only took one variable from a group and ignored the remaining
variables”(Tarun & Sriramya, 2026). However, despite this specific
limitation, across multiple studies LASSO proves to be consistent in its
ability to minimize prediction error and utilize mathematical frameworks
to isolate key critical variables.

For both regression methods, the regularization parameter () was
selected using a 10-fold cross validation, ensuring a practical 90/10
train-test split. Within the function, the dataset is divided into ten
equally sized subsets, where nine folds are used to train the model
while the remaining fold is reserved for validation (testing). The
process is repeated until each fold has served as the validation set
exactly once, and the resulting validation errors are averaged to
estimate the model's predictive performance. By evaluating each
candidate value of the regularization parameter () across all ten folds,
what is identified is the value that minimizes the cross-validated
error. The use of 10-fold cross-validation provides a balance between
computational efficiency and reliable model evaluation, allowing nearly
all observations to contribute to both model training and validation
throughout the procedure, without heightened risk of overfitting or
underfitting.

To evaluate the predictive performance of the Lasso and Ridge regression
models, RMSE, MAE, and R² were calculated using the cross-validated
predictions. RMSE and MAE quantify the average prediction error, with
RMSE placing greater emphasis on larger errors due to the squared error
term, while MAE provides a measure that is less sensitive to extreme
observations. R², however, measures the proportion of variation in the
response variable explained by the model. Lower RMSE and MAE values,
coupled with a higher R², indicate a model that more effectively
balances prediction accuracy with the coefficient shrinkage introduced
through regularization.

The R programming language was the primary software used in this
analysis. Data for the analysis was sourced from the nflreadr package; a
repository of NFL data easily accessible within the R environment. For
data preprocessing, the Python programming language package pandas was
used within the R environment, as well as the native dplyr package. The
corrplot, car, and ggplot2 packages were used for the preliminary
analysis and analysis of the final models. Computation and selection of
the LASSO and Ridge-based models was completed through use of glmnet.

\

- Detail the models or algorithms used.

- Justify your choices based on the problem and data.

*The common non-parametric regression model is*
$Y_i = m(X_i) + \varepsilon_i$*, where* $Y_i$ *can be defined as the sum
of the regression function value* $m(x)$ *for* $X_i$*. Here* $m(x)$ *is
unknown and* $\varepsilon_i$ *some errors. With the help of this
definition, we can create the estimation for local averaging i.e.*
$m(x)$ *can be estimated with the product of* $Y_i$ *average and* $X_i$
*is near to* $x$*. In other words, this means that we are discovering
the line through the data points with the help of surrounding data
points. The estimation formula is printed below [@R-base]:*

$$M_n(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_n (X_i) Y_i  \tag{1}$$ $W_n(x)$ *is the sum
of weights that belongs to all real numbers. Weights are positive
numbers and small if* $X_i$ *is far from* $x$*.*

*Another equation:*

$$y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 +\varepsilon_i$$

## Analysis and Results

### Data Exploration and Visualization

- Describe your data sources and collection process.

- Present initial findings and insights through visualizations.

- Highlight unexpected patterns or anomalies.

A study was conducted to determine how...

The following features are exhibited within the dataset:

- **player_display_name: Name of quarterback for that observation**

- **season: League year the observation represents**

- **team: NFL franchise played for that year by the quarterback**

- **passing_yards: Total number of yards gained via the respective
  quarterback’s passing**

- **passing_tds: Total number of touchdowns scored via the respective
  quarterback’s passing**

- **interceptions: Total number of turnsovers committed via the
  respective quarterback’s passing**

- **completions: Total number of completed passes via the quarterback**

- **attempts: Total number of attempted passes via the quarterback**

- **passing_epa: Average expected points added per passing play,
  measuring how much a team's passing offense increases or decreases its
  expected scoring. Positive values indicate more efficient passing
  performance**

- **rushing_yards: Total number of yards gained via the respective
  quarterback’s running**

- **rushing_tds: Total number of touchdowns scored via the respective
  quarterback’s running**

- **completion_pct: proportion of completion divided by attempts, as a
  percentage**

- **wins: The sole response variable of our analysis, representing total
  number of wins the quarterback’s team recorded in the respective
  season**

```{r, warning=FALSE, echo=T, message=FALSE}
# loading packages 
library(corrplot)
library(nflreadr)
library(dplyr)
```

```{python}
import pandas as pd
```

```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, echo=TRUE}
# Load Data

years = 2015:2024
weekly = load_player_stats(years)
qbs <- weekly %>%
  filter(position == "QB")
qb_season <- qbs %>%
  group_by(player_display_name, season, team) %>%
  summarize(
    passing_yards = sum(passing_yards, na.rm = TRUE),
    passing_tds = sum(passing_tds, na.rm = TRUE),
    interceptions = sum(passing_interceptions, na.rm = TRUE),
    completions = sum(completions, na.rm = TRUE),
    attempts = sum(attempts, na.rm = TRUE),
    passing_epa = sum(passing_epa, na.rm = TRUE),
    rushing_yards = sum(rushing_yards, na.rm = TRUE),
    rushing_tds = sum(rushing_tds, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

qb_season <- qb_season %>%
  mutate(
    completion_pct = (completions / attempts) * 100
  )

write.csv(qb_season, "qb_season_stats.csv", row.names = FALSE)

schedules <- load_schedules(2015:2024)
team_wins <- schedules %>%
  filter(game_type == "REG") %>%
  mutate(
    home_win = ifelse(home_score > away_score, 1, 0),
    away_win = ifelse(away_score > home_score, 1, 0)
  )

home_wins <- team_wins %>%
  group_by(season, team = home_team) %>%
  summarize(wins = sum(home_win, na.rm = TRUE))

away_wins <- team_wins %>%
  group_by(season, team = away_team) %>%
  summarize(wins = sum(away_win, na.rm = TRUE))

team_total_wins <- bind_rows(home_wins, away_wins) %>%
  group_by(season, team) %>%
  summarize(wins = sum(wins))

qb_season <- qb_season %>%
  left_join(team_total_wins,
            by = c("season", "team"))

write.csv(qb_season,
          "qb_season_dataset.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)

qb_season <- qb_season %>%
  filter(attempts >= 200)


write.csv(qb_season,
          "qb_season_dataset.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)

qb_season1 <- qb_season %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(-player_display_name, -season, -team)
```

```{r}
correlation_matrix = round(cor(qb_season1, use = "complete.obs"), 2)
corrplot(correlation_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper",
         tl.col = "black", tl.srt = 45)
```

Figure 1: Correlation Plot between Variables

According to the correlation plot above, the passing EPA has the
strongest correlation with team wins (r = 0.66). Completion percentage
showed a moderate correlation with wins (r=0.41). The rushing statistics
like rushing touchdowns and rushing yards appeared to have the lowest
positive relationship with wins (average r= 0.22) while interceptions
had almost no linear relationship with wins (r=0.06). 

The analysis also identified substantial multicollinearity among several
passing statistics. Completions and attempts were nearly perfectly
correlated (r = 0.99), while passing yards showed very strong
correlations with completions (r = 0.97) and attempts (r = 0.96). These
findings suggest that some passing variables may provide redundant
information and should be carefully considered during model
development. 

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(qb_season1, aes(x = passing_yards, y = wins)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Passing Yards vs. Team Wins",
    x = "Passing Yards",
    y = "Team Wins"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
ggplot(qb_season1, aes(x = passing_tds, y = wins)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", se = TRUE) +
  labs(
    title = "Passing Touchdowns vs. Team Wins",
    x = "Passing Touchdowns",
    y = "Team Wins"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

Both scatterplots show that passing yards and passing touchdowns have a
relative strong positive correlation to team wins. These scatterplots
show that both variables, passing yards and passing touchdowns have a
positive effect on team wins. The passing touchdowns versus team wins
scatterplot shows a stronger relationship with less variability and
spread in the data comparatively to passing yards versus team wins. The
conclusion can be made that as passing touchdowns increase, team wins
also increase and similarly as passing yards increase, team wins
increase. This conclusion is beneficial in highlightly possible key
variables that strongly influence team wins. \

```{r}
VIF_Visualization_Model <- lm(wins ~ passing_yards + passing_tds + interceptions + completions + attempts + passing_epa + rushing_yards + rushing_tds + completion_pct, data = qb_season1)
library(car)
VIF_Visualization_Values <- vif(VIF_Visualization_Model)
VIF_Visualization_Values
VIF_Visualization_Dataframe <- data.frame(Variable = names(VIF_Visualization_Values), VIF = as.numeric(VIF_Visualization_Values))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(VIF_Visualization_Dataframe, aes(x = reorder(Variable, VIF), y = VIF)) + geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + geom_hline(yintercept = 5, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") + coord_flip() + labs(title = "Variance Inflation Factors (VIFs) of Dataset Predictors", x = "Predictor", y = "VIF") + theme_minimal()

```

Variance Inflation Factors (VIFs) of the predictors against the response
variable (wins) are modeled based on a simple linear regression created
for the purposes of this visualization. To combat inflated variances due
to predictor multicollinearity, a VIF threshold of 5 was used, exhibited
by the red-dashed line running along the y-axis. Predictors such as
interceptions, rushing_tds, and rushing_yards satisfy this threshold.
Although completion_pct, passing_tds, and passing_epa do not meet the
threshold, they are close enough to at least consider in a final model
if other evidence supports their inclusion. Completions, attempts, and
passing_yards, however, indicate such high variances that they should
likely not be used when refining a model.

### Modeling and Results

- Explain your data preprocessing and cleaning steps.

- Present your key findings in a clear and concise manner.

- Use visuals to support your claims.

- **Tell a story about what the data reveals.**

```{r}

```

### Conclusion

- Summarize your key findings.

- Discuss the implications of your results.

## References

Gary C. McDonald. (2009). Ridge regression. WIREs Computational
Statistics, 1(1), 93–100. <https://doi.org/10.1002/wics.14>

Robert Tibshirani (1996). Regression shrinkage and selection via the
lasso. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B
(Methodological), 58(1), 267–288.
<https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2517-6161.1996.tb02080.x>

Gweon, J. (2024). Comparing different machine learning methods on NFL
defensive player performance prediction \[Master's report, Kansas State
University\]. K-State Research
Exchange.<https://krex.k-state.edu/items/b5cae4fb-f772-42ce-9dad-d7a84094e8bf> 

Juuri, S. (2023). Predicting the results of NFL games using machine
learning \[Master's thesis, Aalto University\].
Aaltodoc.<https://aaltodoc.aalto.fi/items/8a329777-bdfe-4800-8d8d-d6dc3e75be70> 

South, C., & Egros, E. (2020). Forecasting college football game
outcomes using modern modeling techniques. Journal of Sports Analytics,
6(1), 25-33.

Venkat Sai Tarun G, Sriramya P. Analyzing ola data for precise price
prediction using xgboost technique comparing with lasso regression. In:
Advances in Parallel Computing Algorithms, Tools and Paradigms. SAGE
Publications; 2026:360-366.
doi:[10.3233/APC220050](https://doi.org/10.3233/APC220050)

Laufer, B., Docherty, P. D., Murray, R., Krueger-Ziolek, S., Jalal, N.
A., Hoeflinger, F., Rupitsch, S. J., Reindl, L., & Moeller, K. (2023).
Sensor Selection for Tidal Volume Determination via Linear
Regression—Impact of Lasso versus Ridge Regression. Sensors (Basel,
Switzerland), 23(17), 7407. <https://doi.org/10.3390/s23177407>

Geraldo-Campos, L. A., Soria, J. J., & Pando-Ezcurra, T. (2022). Machine
learning for credit risk in the Reactive Peru Program: A comparison of
the Lasso and Ridge Regression models. Economies, 10(8), 1–21.
<https://doi.org/10.3390/economies10080188>

Alshqaq, S. S., & Abuzaid, A. H. (2023). An efficient method for
variable selection based on diagnostic-Lasso regression. Symmetry,
15(12), 2155.<https://doi.org/10.3390/sym15122155>

Jana, A., & Hemalatha, S. (2021). Football player performance analysis
using particle swarm optimization and player value calculation using
regression. Journal of Physics: Conference Series, 1911(1), 012011.
<https://doi.org/10.1088/1742-6596/1911/1/012011>

Lockhart, R., Taylor, J., Tibshirani, R. J., & Tibshirani, R. (2014). A
significance test for the lasso. The Annals of Statistics, 42(2),
413–468. <https://doi.org/10.1214/13-AOS1175>

Bhattacharyya, J. (2025, January 23). LASSO regression – A procedural
improvement (SSRN Working Paper No. 5108591). SSRN.
https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5108591

\

\
